Off Calendar May-15-2025 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES:

Hearing Re: PLAINTIFF DEBORAH HIGER's Motion To Compel Responses To Requests For Production Of Documents And For Monetary Sanctions.

No appearances made in this matter. The Court adopts its tentative ruling.

The court previously heard this motion and set a further briefing and hearing schedule. No party has filed updates. Accordingly, the court assumes that all remaining discovery disputes concerning Plaintiff's motion to compel further responses to her requests for production of documents have been resolved. The court previously put over the issue of sanctions. (See Order, May 7, 2025, at 4:28-5:1.) The court finds that Dignity's opposition to Plaintiff's motion lacked substantial justification in part in its overreading of Evidence Code 1157 but reduces the sanctions award to reflect that the parties have resolved their remaining disputes concerning this motion. The court awards $1000 in sanctions, payable by Dignity Health to Plaintiff, within 30 days of entry of this order.

Counsel for the prevailing party is to prepare and submit a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the court's ruling, with a cover letter demonstrating compliance with CRC 3.1312.

Judge: Christine Van Aken; Clerk: V. Da Fonseca; Not reported. (301/CVA)

Hearing is held. Discussed, not ARGUED. The Court adopts the tentative ruling as follows:

DEFENDANTS DR. DAVID HOLST, M.D., DR. RONALD K. LAGRO, M.D., and DIGNITIY HEALTH'S Motion To Compel Non-Party Shasta View Nursing Center'S Compliance With Subpoena.

Defendants Dignity Health, Dr. David Holst, and Dr. Ronald K. Lagro move to compel non-party Shasta View Nursing Center ("Shasta View") to comply with Defendants' subpoena of records. No opposition was filed. The court denies the motion.

Defendants served a subpoena on Shasta View seeking the personnel file for plaintiff Dr. Deborah Higer, who testified to her employment there. (Baca Dec. Exs. B, C.) Shasta View eventually submitted a declaration under penalty of perjury stating that it has no payroll or employment records for Higer. (Ex. E.) Defendants believe Higer's discovery responses demonstrate that that cannot be correct. But the response is full and complete in itself, and sworn under penalty of perjury. The court agrees that it is a puzzling response (either May was untruthful, Higer was untruthful, Shasta View didn't keep employment or payment records, Shasta View destroyed its employment records, or similar farfetched possibilities), and Defendants may well have justification to seek, for instance, deposition testimony from the declarant or a Shasta View PMQ to explain the puzzling response. But the discovery statutes do not provide a basis for the court to test the truth of complete and nonevasive responses.

The court does not award sanctions.

Counsel for the moving party is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling in compliance with CRC 3.1312.

MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT In The Alternative, Summary Adjudication
Off Calendar Oct-23-2024 Denied
MINI MINUTES:

HEARING RE: DEFENDANT DR. DAVID HOLST, M.D., DR. RONALD K. LAGRO, M.D., AND DIGNITIY HEALTH's MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT In The Alternative, Summary Adjudication.

No appearances made in this matter. The Court adopts its tentative ruling.

Defendants' motion for summary judgment or alternatively, summary adjudication is denied without prejudice.

First, the depositions of two key witnesses (Dr. Holst and Dr. Lagro) have yet to occur. Defendant heavily relies on the doctors' declarations to support this motion. Since department 206 recently vacated the trial date, the court believes that their deposition testimony should be part of the record.

Second, defendants' separate statement does not comply with CRC 3.1342(h). Defendants initially list sixty facts (numbered one through sixty) presumably to obtain summary judgment. Defendants then list thirty-seven facts to obtain summary adjudication on the first cause of action for discrimination. Those facts are numbered one through thirty-seven. The defect in defendants' motion is that serially numbered facts one through thirty-seven in support of summary judgment are different from facts one through thirty-seven in support of summary adjudication. When one refers to undisputed material fact one, it should be the same fact. Several of defendants' facts also violate Reeves v. Safeway Stores, Inc. (2004) 121 Cal.App.4th 95, 105-106. For example, facts thirty-seven through thirty-nine in support of summary judgment do not "plainly and concisely" list material facts per CCP 437c(b)(1). Rather, they merely parrot plaintiff's interrogatory responses. Discovery responses are not "material facts." Rather, discovery responses are evidence to support a material fact.

One issue in this case is whether plaintiff was an employee who can rely on FEHA. (See Vernon v. State of California (2004) 116 Cal.App.4th 114, 123 ["The FEHA, however, prohibits only 'an employer' from engaging in improper discrimination."]; Sistare-Meyer v. Young Men's Christian Assn. (1997) 58 Cal.App.4th 10, 17 [FEHA's antidiscrimination provisions do not apply to independent contractors].). It appears that several of the FEHA claims are not viable because plaintiff admits that she was an independent contractor. (Complaint, par. 18; Baca Decl., Ex. 6 [response to request for admission 11 ("Admit YOU have only performed work for Defendant as an independent contractor.")].) The admission is conclusive. (Murillo v. Superior Court (2006) 143 Cal.App.4th 730, 736 ["As a general rule an admission is conclusive in the action as to the party making it. [citations] Absent leave of court to amend or withdraw the admission, no contradictory evidence may be introduced."].) In any event, this issue can be explored if defendants renew a code compliant motion.

Counsel for the prevailing party is required to prepare and submit a proposed order in compliance with CRC 3.1312.
